title: Amp (Amp Simulation)
summary: Simulates a guitar/bass amplifier — adds tube harmonics, cabinet colouration, and amp character. More tonal colour than Saturator. Used in Kettama for bass warmth. Suite only.
steps: Add Amp to a bass or synth track (in Ableton Live Suite — not available in Standard)
Choose Amp Type: Clean = gentle saturation, warm. Ext Bass = enhanced low-end response. Bass = focused on bass frequencies. Heavy = aggressive gain with scooped mids
Drive: overall input gain into the amp. 20–40% for warmth. 60%+ for overdrive character
Bass, Middle, Treble: amp tone controls. These interact like a real amp — they're not independent EQ bands but influence each other
Cabinet: choose the speaker cabinet simulation (affects the high-end character significantly)
Dry/Wet: blend the amp-processed signal with the original. 50–80% for warmth with some original clarity
tips: Amp vs Saturator: Amp has more character and tonal colouration (cabinet simulation, tone stack). Saturator is more precise and controlled. Use Amp when you want the sound to feel 'played through gear'
Amp is Suite only — if you have Live Standard, use Saturator in Analog Clip mode for a similar (if less characterful) effect
Ext Bass type with low Drive (15%) adds low-mid warmth to a sub bass without distorting it — similar to Saturator but with a more analogue character
The Cabinet toggle (Cab on/off) changes the sound dramatically — with Cabinet off, Amp is just a drive and tone stage. With Cabinet on, it adds the speaker resonance colouration
practice_hint: Amp is Suite-only. If you have it: add Amp to a Reese bass track. Set type to Bass, Drive 30%, Dry/Wet 60%. Compare with Saturator at similar settings — hear the different character each device adds.
ableton_version_note: Amp is available in Ableton Live Suite only. Not available in Standard.
